title: Wet-felted hat on a hat block
subtitle: A brimless crown hat shaped over a wooden or polystyrene block
excerpt: Felt a cone of merino roving around a hat-shaped resist, remove the resist when the felt has set, then block the wet hat over a head-shaped block to set the final crown shape and rim. This produces a firm, seamless hat with a naturally shaped crown.
sourceNotes: The hat-felting tradition runs through the British felting trades. The hand-felting of wool hat blanks is documented in trade accounts from the 17th century onward. The contemporary home version using a polystyrene head-form resist draws on the same principle without the industrial felting machinery. Observation-led method.

content.text: A felt hat is made in two stages: felting a rough hollow form over a simple flat-oval
, then blocking the wet felt form over a head-shaped

to set the final crown profile. The resist stage does the structural work; the block stage sets the shape. This tutorial produces a brimless crown hat that fits a 56-58 cm head circumference. Use 150-180 g of merino for a medium-weight hat.

items.name: Flat oval resist, 30 x 22 cm, cut from dense foam or thick plastic sheet
Hat block or polystyrene head form (56-58 cm circumference)

items.qty: in a large bucket or basin
items.name: Hot water (60-65°C)

items.qty: for checking head circumference against the hat opening
items.name: Soft tape measure

content.text: Step 1: Build the roving around the resist

Lay the flat oval resist on the bamboo mat. Cover one face with four cross-hatched layers of roving, extending 8-10 cm beyond the resist edge on all sides (this excess forms the crown of the hat above the resist oval). Wet and partially felt the first face before flipping and adding four layers to the other face, again with 8-10 cm excess at the top. The excess on both faces meets above the resist and forms the hat crown; the resist forms the head opening area.
Step 2: Felt both faces and the crown

Rub all surfaces for 30-40 minutes, flipping regularly and paying particular attention to the crown area above the resist where the two excess layers must join. The crown join is the most important structural point in the hat. Test it: the crown roving should not peel apart when you pinch and pull it. Once both faces and the crown pass the pinch test, the structural felt is ready.
Step 3: Remove the resist and cut the opening

Slide the resist toward the open edge of the hat and cut a slit to extract it. You now have a rough hollow hat with a ragged brim. Trim the brim to a clean oval edge, or fold it up and needle-felt it into a rolled brim. Rub the cut edge firmly with soapy fingers to bind any raw fibres.
Step 4: Block the hat over the head form

While the hat is still wet, pull it over the hat block or polystyrene head form. The brim opening should be slightly smaller than the head form at this point: stretch it firmly over the block. Smooth the crown with your palms to eliminate any bulges, and pull the felt evenly down the sides of the block. Leave on the block to dry completely (several hours). Once dry, the hat will hold the block's shape permanently.

items.fix: Continue rolling and rubbing the hat to full it further before trying the block again. Measure the brim opening after each full session and compare to the head circumference you are aiming for.
items.cause: Insufficient fulling before blocking. The hat needs to be felted to a size slightly smaller than the target before blocking stretches it to shape.
items.symptom: Hat is too large for the block after blocking

items.fix: While the hat is still wet and on the block, rub the crown join firmly with soapy fingers. Then roll the crown area in a small piece of bubble wrap for 2-3 minutes. The visible join should fill in as the fibres full together.
items.cause: The crown join was not felted firmly enough during the resist stage.
items.symptom: Hat has a seam line at the crown where the two faces joined
